talk_title: Epigenomic heterogeneity and chromatin variants in metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer

speaker.name: Matthew Loupiain
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Medicine

models.name: 71 mCRPC patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: LINE1+, SETL, SINE1+, constant subgroups; AR amplification in LINE1+

clinical_data.population: LINE1+ mCRPC patients treated with AR selective inhibitors

MOA_summary: Chromatin variant analysis using ATAC‑seq identified repetitive element‑driven accessible regions that recruit AR, FOXA1 and other transcription factors, forming de novo regulatory elements that amplify AR expression in LINE1+ metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer, leading to intrinsic resistance to AR‑targeted therapy.
key_findings: Unsupervised clustering of ATAC‑seq data from 71 mCRPC samples defined four subgroups based on repetitive element chromatin variants (LINE1+, SETL, SINE1+, constant).
LINE1+ subgroup showed intrinsic resistance to androgen‑receptor selective inhibitors, with markedly shorter survival.
AR locus amplification (up to >150 copies) and ecDNA positivity were enriched in LINE1+ tumors.
Repetitive element‑derived accessible sites in LINE1+ tumors physically interact with the AR promoter (Hi‑C), recruiting AR, FOXA1, and OCTB13.
Chromatin variant classification did not align with gene‑expression‑based subtypes, highlighting orthogonal heterogeneity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LINE1+ tumors are intrinsically resistant to AR‑selective inhibitors due to extreme AR overexpression driven by repeat‑derived enhancers.
open_questions: Do repeat‑derived chromatin variants directly generate ecDNA, or are they a consequence of ecDNA formation?
What upstream mechanisms (e.g., p53 loss, metabolic stress) drive the emergence of LINE1+ chromatin variant profiles?

talk_title: SMARCA4 loss drives cell‑state disorder and dedifferentiated endometrial cancer

speaker.name: Unspecified presenter (endometrial cancer work)
speaker.affiliation: University of Alberta, Department of Oncology

models.name: KRAS‑LKB1 mouse lung adenocarcinoma organoids (used for parallel studies)
microdissected well‑differentiated vs dedifferentiated regions of DDEC
models.genotype_or_key_features: SMARCA4 loss, ARID1B loss in dedifferentiated component

models.name: SMARCA4‑KO and WT endometrial cancer cells serially passaged in mice

MOA_summary: Loss of SMARCA4 disrupts the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, leading to global DNA hypomethylation and stochastic chromatin accessibility changes that prevent cells from stabilizing in a differentiated state, resulting in histologic dedifferentiation and therapy resistance in endometrial cancer.
key_findings: 80% of dedifferentiated endometrial cancer (DDEC) cases show loss of SMARCA4 and/or ARID1B, components of the SWI/SNF complex.

CRISPR‑mediated SMARCA4 knockout in endometrial cancer cells reduces in‑vitro tumorigenicity but, after serial in‑vivo passaging, restores growth and yields mixed well‑ and poorly‑differentiated histology.
SMARCA4‑deficient tumors display global DNA hypomethylation and distinct chromatin accessibility patterns compared with wild‑type.
Single‑cell multi‑omics reveal that SMARCA4 loss increases cell‑state plasticity, generating an uncommitted transcriptional state without a stem‑cell or embryonic signature.
Patient DDEC samples retain heterogeneous E‑cadherin expression, supporting a model of cell‑state disorder rather than true dedifferentiation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SMARCA4‑deficient tumors are resistant to carboplatin chemotherapy in vivo.
open_questions: What upstream signals (e.g., hypoxia, metabolic stress) trigger SMARCA4 loss‑induced chromatin variants?
Can epigenetic drugs restore chromatin stability and re‑sensitize SMARCA4‑deficient DDEC to chemotherapy?

talk_title: LKB1 loss‑driven adenocarcinoma‑to‑squamous transition (AST) and therapeutic vulnerabilities in KRAS‑mutant lung cancer

speaker.name: Hongjun Ji
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: KLK6A
biomarkers.finding: high KLK6A expression correlates with shorter treatment duration and poor survival in LKB1‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma

models.name: KRAS‑G12D; LKB1‑KO (KL) genetically engineered mouse model
CHRISTOPHER‑1 trial lung cancer samples (n≈8)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12C or EGFR mutant, LKB1 status known

clinical_data.value: shorter in patients with high squamous signature

MOA_summary: LKB1 loss elevates ROS, transiently activating NF‑κB (RELA) which sustains adenocarcinoma survival; prolonged stress leads to a double‑positive p63⁺/RELA⁺ state that secretes IL‑1α, reprograms CAFs to a leukocyte‑like phenotype, induces IL‑1β‑driven neutrophil NET formation, and drives squamous transdifferentiation (AST) that confers resistance to KRAS‑G12C inhibition; combined NF‑κB inhibition, ROS modulation, and IL‑1 blockade synergize with KRAS‑G12C inhibitor to prevent or eradicate squamous lesions.
key_findings: LKB1 loss in KRAS‑mutant mouse models induces spontaneous adenocarcinoma‑to‑squamous transition (AST) that is enriched after targeted therapy.
High squamous transcriptional signature predicts shorter treatment duration in LKB1‑mutant lung cancer patients.
KLK6A expression serves as a predictive biomarker for poor response to aggressive therapy in LKB1‑deficient tumors.
ROS accumulation activates NF‑κB; NF‑κB inhibition accelerates p63⁺ squamous differentiation, while ROS scavenging (NAC) reduces RELA activation.
p63⁺ squamous cells secrete IL‑1α, reprogramming CAFs to IL‑1β‑producing leukocyte‑like CAFs that trigger neutrophil NET formation, promoting squamous tumor growth.
Combined KRAS‑G12C inhibitor (IMC‑6236) with IL‑1 receptor antagonist or NET inhibitor (GSK‑484) markedly reduces squamous tumor burden and improves overall tumor control.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AST confers resistance to KRAS‑G12C inhibition; NF‑κB activation maintains adenocarcinoma lineage but its inhibition promotes squamous transition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target oncogenic KRAS while preventing emergence of resistant squamous phenotype
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor regression of adenocarcinoma; squamous lesions persist unless combined with IL‑1 blockade

combination_strategies.rationale: Block IL‑1α/β signaling that drives CAF reprogramming and NET formation supporting squamous growth
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced squamous tumor number and NETs in KRAS‑LKB1 mouse model

combination_strategies.rationale: Transient NF‑κB activity sustains adenocarcinoma; inhibition accelerates AST, suggesting timing matters for combination
combination_strategies.phenotype: accelerated p63⁺ squamous differentiation; reduced adenocarcinoma survival in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit neutrophil extracellular trap formation that supports squamous tumor growth
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased squamous tumor burden and NET levels

open_questions: What determines the threshold of ROS/NF‑κB activation that switches from protective to pro‑AST signaling?
Can early detection of the double‑positive p63⁺/RELA⁺ state predict imminent squamous transition in patients?
